summary: This paper develops a high-dimensional theoretical analysis of how quantized linear models trained with the straight-through estimator (STE) learn. Working in a teacher-student linear regression setup with isotropic Gaussian data and uniform, component-wise quantization applied to the weights (and, in the joint case, also to the inputs), the authors show that in the online/one-pass regime and the high-dimensional limit (d to infinity), the microscopic distribution of the parameters is governed by an SDE/PDE, while a small set of macroscopic order parameters obeys a closed, deterministic system of ODEs. Building on this reduction, they study how the quantization hyperparameters, bit-width and quantization range, shape the learning trajectory. The analysis predicts a characteristic two-phase behavior in which the generalization error sits on a plateau before dropping sharply, with the plateau length controlled by the quantization range; it also predicts non-monotonic or oscillatory transients under low-bit input quantization and, through a fixed-point and stability analysis, quantifies the asymptotic deviation from the unquantized model, including regimes where quantization behaves as a regularizer and can even enlarge the stable learning region. Finite-dimensional STE simulations are provided and track the ODE predictions closely. Methodologically, a notable feature is the joint treatment of weight and input quantization, which requires extending high-dimensional SGD analysis to nonlinear transformations of both the weights and the inputs.
strengths_and_weaknesses:
On soundness, the theoretical development is careful and, to the extent I could check, rigorous: the passage from the microscopic SDE/PDE description to the deterministic ODEs for the order parameters is handled cleanly, the assumptions are stated explicitly, and the fixed-point and stability analysis is a natural and useful complement. The finite-dimensional simulations are consistent with the predicted curves, which lends credibility to the reduction. My main reservation is that the closed-form ODE result (Theorem 5.3) hinges on the closure in Assumption 5.1, an isotropic Gaussian closure on the component of the weight vector orthogonal to the teacher, which is neither formally justified nor directly verified against the finite-dimensional simulations. Relatedly, the isotropic Gaussian input assumption is motivated in part by universality, but since quantization applies a strong pointwise nonlinearity it is not obvious that universality should survive, and the robustness of the conclusions to non-Gaussian, correlated, or otherwise structured inputs is not examined. I would also flag the use of an all-ones teacher in some experiments: because component-wise quantization breaks the spherical symmetry that would normally make this gauge choice harmless, an all-ones teacher concentrates mass into a single bin type, and a generic teacher spreading mass across bins could plausibly change the plateau and fixed-point behavior.

On presentation, the paper is well organized and, despite genuinely technical material, largely readable; the derivations are laid out in a logical order and the main results are clearly stated. The exposition is nonetheless dense in places and would benefit from more connective, intuition-building text and, where possible, illustrative diagrams around the core construction (in particular the material between the main definition and the central theorem). There are also language and formatting issues, grammatical slips and spelling mistakes, that should be cleaned up. The figures comparing theory and simulation could be more convincing if the simulated trajectories were shown as curves over the full time axis with error bars, which would make the agreement of the transition or switch times explicit rather than leaving it to sampled points. Finally, a fair amount of the early paper is devoted to notation and prior work, and the broader-impact discussion is uninformative.

On significance, the work targets a real and comparatively under-studied question: rather than focusing on surrogate-gradient design or asymptotic convergence, it asks how bit-width and quantization range shape the entire learning trajectory, and it does so within a single framework that handles both weight and input quantization. The qualitative predictions, the plateau-then-drop trajectory, the range-dependence of the plateau, non-monotonic transients, quantization-as-regularization, and the implied guidance on when early stopping helps, are interesting and could motivate more principled hyperparameter choices. The principal limitation on significance is the distance between this stylized setting and the regimes where STE is actually deployed. It is unclear how predictive the identified phenomena are for quantization-aware training of deep networks, and the empirical validation stays within idealized synthetic conditions that are closely matched to the theory; there is no test of whether the behavior transfers to more realistic models or data. Even modest corroboration on shallow STE-trained networks would considerably strengthen the case for practical relevance.

On originality, bringing high-dimensional online-learning dynamics to bear on STE-based quantized training is a worthwhile and non-obvious move, and the extension to nonlinear transformations of both weights and inputs is a genuine technical step beyond analyses that quantize only one of the two. At the same time, it is not always easy to see which parts of the derivation required genuinely new ideas as opposed to careful adaptation of established high-dimensional and SGD machinery; the paper would be stronger if it explicitly delineated its methodological novelties. I would also note that regression with squared loss, while a standard and convenient setting, may not be the most natural target for quantization, in classification one might hope to quantize while preserving labels, whereas here quantization introduces a non-removable bias, so it is worth clarifying whether this choice is dictated by technical obstacles or by scope.

Overall, this is a mathematically solid and genuinely interesting contribution to the theory of STE-based quantized training, with clear value on the theoretical side, held back mainly by an unverified closure assumption, a limitations discussion that is essentially absent, and a gap between the stylized model and practice that the current experiments do not attempt to bridge.
key_questions_for_authors:
1. Can you provide numerical evidence, even at small scale, that the predicted phenomena, the plateau followed by a sharp drop and the non-monotonic transients, also arise in basic quantization-aware training, e.g., shallow networks trained with STE? If not, can you argue for or against why you expect these behaviors to transfer?
2. How robust are the dynamics to the choice of teacher and input distribution? In particular, do the same qualitative behaviors hold for an i.i.d. Gaussian teacher rather than an all-ones teacher, and for non-isotropic or non-Gaussian inputs, given that component-wise quantization breaks spherical symmetry and the pointwise nonlinearity may undermine the universality argument?
3. Under what conditions is the closure in Assumption 5.1 accurate? Can it be verified directly in the finite-dimensional simulations, is it guaranteed for particular model classes, and where might it break down?
4. Is the restriction to linear regression with squared loss driven by technical obstacles or by scope? Do you expect the same qualitative dynamics in a classification setting such as logistic regression, which may be a more natural target for quantization?
5. Could the framework accommodate non-uniform bin spacing (e.g., bfloat16-style grids), and could you make explicit which parts of the derivation are genuinely new versus adaptations of existing high-dimensional dynamics techniques?
limitations: The limitations are not discussed openly, and this should be remedied. At a minimum, the paper should state plainly that its conclusions hold under a restrictive set of conditions, a linear Gaussian teacher-student model, the high-dimensional one-pass regime, uniform scalar quantization, and the closure of Assumption 5.1, and should acknowledge that for deep networks, non-Gaussian or correlated data, and multi-epoch training the predictions remain heuristic rather than validated. A brief, candid paragraph in the conclusion would appropriately set reader expectations.
